coredata.dc:title: Green fabrication of zinc oxide supported carbon dots for visible light-responsive photocatalytic decolourization of Malachite Green dye: Optimization and kinetic studies
coredata.dc:creator: Sekar, Anithadevi
Yadav, Rakhi
coredata.dc:description: A succession of zinc oxide supported carbon dots (CZnO-dots) was fabricated from gum ghatti via a single-pot and straightforward microwave process. The as-fabricated CZnO-dots were employed as the visible-light responsive photocatalyst for the effectual decolourization of Malachite Green dye (MG). The optical band gap was evaluated using a tauc plot in which integration of ZnO to Carbon dots (C-dots) reduces the bandgap compared to bare C-dots and pure ZnO nanoparticles. The increased photocatalytic efficacy was observed in 1 wt% ZnO of CZnO-dots amongst all samples. By the influence of different operating conditions, the CZnO-dots show a degradation efficiency of about 94.8% with an irradiation time of 60 min. Besides capturing the active species in photodegraded products, the possible mechanism behind the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye has been discussed. Notably, the environmental assessment has been measured to develop mung bean plants using treated dye water. The growth of mung bean plants in the treated dye water was almost the same as that found in the control group, while the retarded growth was seen in the MG dye solution. Thus, the as-prepared CZnO-dots can be effectively used for the practical application related to environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Green fabrication of zinc oxide supported carbon dots for visible light-responsive photocatalytic decolourization of Malachite Green dye: Optimization and kinetic studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Madras Christian College, Affiliated to the University of Madras, East Tambaram, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Madras Christian College, Affiliated to the University of Madras

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of chemistry, Madras Christian College, Affiliated to the University of Madras, East Tambaram, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A succession of zinc oxide supported carbon dots (CZnO-dots) was fabricated from gum ghatti via a single-pot and straightforward microwave process. The as-fabricated CZnO-dots were employed as the visible-light responsive photocatalyst for the effectual decolourization of Malachite Green dye (MG). The optical band gap was evaluated using a tauc plot in which integration of ZnO to Carbon dots (C-dots) reduces the bandgap compared to bare C-dots and pure ZnO nanoparticles. The increased photocatalytic efficacy was observed in 1 wt% ZnO of CZnO-dots amongst all samples. By the influence of different operating conditions, the CZnO-dots show a degradation efficiency of about 94.8% with an irradiation time of 60 min. Besides capturing the active species in photodegraded products, the possible mechanism behind the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye has been discussed. Notably, the environmental assessment has been measured to develop mung bean plants using treated dye water. The growth of mung bean plants in the treated dye water was almost the same as that found in the control group, while the retarded growth was seen in the MG dye solution. Thus, the as-prepared CZnO-dots can be effectively used for the practical application related to environmental remediation.

The work presented holds the potential of using visible light-responsive photocatalyst CZnO-dots in hands-on applicability related to environmental remediation.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: The growth of the social structure and the broad area of industrial production worsens urban water contamination [1]. The indiscriminate dumping of industrial effluents has prompted water contamination in many nations. The carcinogenic essence of the wastes comprising dyes originating from garment mills and food factories induce surface and groundwater pollution that presents possible health threats to livestock, plant life, and humans [2]. Malachite Green dye (MG) is a natural triphenylmethane dye mainly employed in silk, wool, leather, and fish farming industries [3,4]. However, several studies asserted that MG dye and its metabolite green leucomalachite (LMG) are potentially obstinate owing to their complicated chemical compositions [5]. MG dye inflows more from municipal sewage treatment plants into the environment and triggers genetic damage [6]. It is also prohibited or limited in several nations. However, owing to its high effectiveness and low cost, MG is still illegal in aquaculture [7]. It is crucial to remove MG residue from water systems efficiently. Several conventional MG dye removal methods from wastewater have been implemented in recent decades [8–10], but such approaches are expensive, laborious, consume high energy, and show poor performance. Likewise, with semiconductor-based photocatalysts, photocatalytic degradation of dyes has induced global interest [11].

Zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) are a potential photocatalyst because they are firmly catalyst, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly [12]. The ZnO NPs nanocatalyst, fast recombination, and wide bandgap (3.37 eV) restrict its applicableness to an extended wavelength region [13]. Visible light active photocatalysts are desirable for the decomposition of harmful chemical pollutants [13,14]. Sufficient modifications are needed to create new ZnO NPs nanostructures to engulf these issues. Carbon dots (C-dots), a zero-dimensional material, have been spotlighted in recent years due to their low cost, non-toxic, photostability, and photoinduced electron transfer behavior [15–17], which is used in versatile applications [18–20]. C-dots turn as an electron pool and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and enhance photocatalytic performance [21]. ZnO NPs coverage on the C-dots could also become a perfect tactic to enhance the charging isolation and hinder photo corrosion [22].

Sharma et al. [23] used chemical precursor ascorbic acid as the carbon source to design N doped ZnO/C-dots nanocatalyst to degrade MG dye aqueous medium. Likewise, Muthulingam et al. [22] fabricated CQD decorated N-doped ZnO via the chemical method to degrade MG with three different light sources (UV, visible, and daylight) and the enhanced performance was found in daylight source. Bozetine et al. [24] utilized fructose and NaOH as precursors to produce ZnO-carbon dots nanocomposites that degrade rhodamine B under visible light irradiation at room temperature. Uthirakumar and co-workers [25] used a wet chemical process (with a minor modification of Hummer's method) to make CQD decorated N-doped ZnO to degrade MG dye with three distinct light sources. Similarly, Several C-dot-based nanocatalysts (like C-dots/Ag/Ag3PO4, C-dots/Fe2O3, C-dots/CuO) are reported [13,26], which significantly suppresses the e-/h+ recombination and expands the spectral response to longer wavelengths.

As mentioned above, earlier literature reports focused on the chemical carbon sources to synthesize metal/metal oxide-C-dots as nanocatalyst for various dye degradation. However, it is hard to find info on green synthesized ZnO/C-dots nanocatalyst scheme degrading MG dyes, particularly under visible light irradiation, leading to sustainable chemistry. Herein, we report a simple green fabrication of CZnO-dots using Gum Ghatti, a natural exudate from tree Anogeissus latifolia as a renewable carbon source through microwave pyrolysis approach. As-prepared CZnO-dots, displaying enhanced photocatalytic behavior of MG dye in the aqueous phase under visible light. A detailed analysis of the different operating parameters of CZnO-dots photocatalytic activity is investigated, such as ZnO addition %, catalyst loading, initial dye concentration, and the dye solution's pH. The percentage of ZnO addition improves the active sites on the surface photocatalysts, and the outcomes are compared with those of pure ZnO NPs and bare C-dots. Finally, the possible mechanism of visible-light-responsive CZnO-dots photodegradation is also proposed based on radical scavenging studies.

Gum ghatti powder was purchased from the State Forest Research Institute, Madhya Pradesh, India. Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn (CH3COO)2.2H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were purchased from Merck, India. Malachite green and ethanol were purchased from LOBA Chemie, India. Double distilled water (DDW) was used for the experiments.

Powder X-ray diffraction patterns of CZnO-dots nanocatalyst were procured from 2θ values (20–80°) via BRUKER USA D8 Advance, Davinci, X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å). The ZnO incorporation of CZnO-dots data was acquired by employing an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) Thermo electron instrument (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) by Al K_X-ray radiation as the source for excitation (1486.8 eV, 500_m). The surface of the prepared nanocatalyst CZnO-dots was imaged using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100F), consuming an acceleration voltage of 200 kV and Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) ((SUPRA 55) CARL ZEISS, Germany). To further confirm the CZnO-dots surface groups Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (SHIMADZU, IRTRACER 100) analysis was taken, equipped with ATR at room temperature in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The optical studies of CZnO-dots were examined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer- Lambda 950, USA) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, LS-45, UK).

CZnO-dots were tailored by a one-step microwave pyrolysis method, corresponding to a formerly existing scheme with slight variations [27]. In brief, 3 g of Gum Ghatti powder was dissolved in 30 ml DDW and held for stirring till the solution was translucent. Then, Zinc acetate by the different weight percent (0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2) of the gum extract was mixed with the above mixture and stirred for 30 mins. It is then shifted to a microwave oven of 80% power for 20 min. The as-obtained blend was aerated at ambient temperature and is centrifuged for 30 min at around 9k rpm. The resultant supernatant solution mixture was dried on the water bath and named CZnO-dots. For comparison, pure ZnO NPs and bare- C-dots were also synthesized using the gum ghatti as the corresponding reducing agent and the carbon source. The as-synthesized CZnO-dots with 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 wt% of ZnO addition was named CZ-1, CZ-2, CZ-3, and CZ-4, respectively.

For decolourization of the MG dye aqueous solution under visible light, the photocatalytic potency of CZnO-dots was assessed. In the self-assembled photoreactor, the reaction was performed consisting of a magnetic stirrer and a visible light source (Philips bulb 23 W) close-fitting chamber. In a standard photocatalytic procedure, 30 ml of 0.05 g/L MG dye solution was taken in the glass beaker, and 1.5 mg of CZnO-dots nanocatalyst was added. Before the visible light irradiation, the above mixture was agitated for 30 min to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium in dark conditions. Further, the reaction was initiated by exposing the dye mixture to the visible light for 60 min since no more degradation was witnessed after that. The distance between the dye mixture and the visible light source was 20 cm. A fixed aliquot was extracted with a syringe and centrifuged at regular intervals. The centrifuged solution was collected in a quartz cuvette, and the absorbance of the MG dye solution was monitored at wavelength 617 nm using a spectrophotometer. Experiments were triplicated for each photocatalysis trials, and its decolourization proficiency was calculated by

A0 and A are the absorbances of MG dye before and after the visible light irradiation. The pH of the MG dye aqueous solution is 8. Under similar experimental conditions, the influence of various operational parameters such as ZnO addition %, CZnO-dots nanocatalyst loading, initial MG dye concentration, and diverse pH range photocatalytic decolourization of MG was also inspected. The photolysis trial of MG dye in the absence of CZnO-dots nanocatalyst under visible light was carried out by procedure. Additionally, the photocatalytic efficiency of Gum Ghatti reduced pure ZnO NPs, and bare C-dots derived from Gum Ghatti were also investigated under visible light and compared with CZnO-dots nanocatalyst. The kinetics rate of decomposition of MG dye for CZnO-dots samples, pure ZnO NPs, and bare C-dots were also studied using Langmuir- Hinshelwood kinetic model

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mung bean seeds germination and plant growth method
ce:section.ce:para: For germination, mung bean seeds (Vigna radiata) were washed with DDW and immersed in the required volume of tap water. After one day, the moist mung bean seeds were gathered and tied in the wet cloth until they sprout. The sprouted mung bean seeds were transferred to the petri dish plates covered with cotton for observing the growth under the different conditions like (a) DDW (b) CZnO-dots as controlled conditions, (c) in pollutant model MG dye, and (d) in treated dye solution (degraded MG dye solution after the photocatalysis by CZnO-dots in the visible light). Three trials were performed for 15 days by adding 3 ml of each respective solution to check the growth of mung bean plants.

Fig. 1a shows the Gum Ghatti derived bare C-dots XRD pattern with the typical broad 2θ peak at 18.1° recognized for the (002) plane, which agrees with graphitic carbon (C-dots). The C-dots d- spacing value was 0.48 nm which was calculated using Bragg law (d = nλ /2 sinθ where, n = 1 positive integer, λ = 1.54 Å wavelength of incident X-ray and θ peak position (002)). The d spacing value is higher on comparing with the "bulk graphite" (0.34–0.35 nm) due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups might raise the interlayer distance or formation of disoriented (turbostratic) carbon atoms [19,20]. Fig. 1b portrays the X-Ray pattern with the characteristics planes (1 0 0), (1 0 1), (0 0 2), (1 1 0), (1 0 3), (2 0 0), (1 0 2) and (1 1 2), which were acquired from diffraction peaks of ZnO NPs [28]. It ratifies the wurtzite ZnO phase's creation with the standard JCPDS pattern (PDF#36-1514). The purity of synthesized pure ZnO NPs was confirmed by the non-existence of peaks from other phases. From the Scherrer equation L = Kλ/ β cosθ (where L, K (=0.9), λ (=1.54 Å), β is the average nanoparticles size, dimensionless shape factor, incident X-ray wavelength, and full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak respectively and θ is the peak position (101)) the mean size of nanoparticles were calculated. The mean size of the fabricated ZnO NPs was 23 nm. The ZnO d- spacing value was 0.24 nm for the peak position (101).

The CZnO- dots samples (Fig. 1 c) (CZ-1, CZ-2, CZ-3, CZ-4) notably exposes a broad hump at 2θ = 19.4° due to the C (002) plane with the interlayer spacing of 0.45 nm [13,27]. Additionally, ZnO NPs peaks were witnessed in accord with the JCPDS card no. 36-1451. As shown in Fig. 1 c, compared to bare C-dots, the two theta values for the CZnO-dots samples changed from about 18 to 19.4°, suggesting that the ZnO was successfully integrated into the carbon lattice. Also, the increasing weight ratio of ZnO addition, indicating the more robust diffraction pattern and change in the lattice distance of ZnO, which may have resulted from the high oxidation state of the Zn [29]. The ZnO NPs d- spacing in the CZnO–dots (CZ-2 sample) was calculated to be 0.25 nm (101 plane peak position), which was slightly higher than the pure ZnO. It might be due to the cleaving of larger ZnO NPs during the CZnO – dots synthesis thru the microwave process [28].

On the ZnO addition, the diffraction peaks assigned to the carbon planes are less intense and broader than the bare C-dots sample suggesting a reduction in the amorphous nature. According to the previous research studies, C-dots supported groups may change the characteristics of the XRD pattern from amorphous to polycrystalline [30]. It might be the outcome of the carbonization of starting materials, resulting in fused polycyclic fragments. Polycrystalline order was achieved in the C-dot system by connecting multiple layers of such fragments through C-dots supported groups. [30]. As a result, in gum ghatti derived CZnO-dot scheme signifying their polycrystalline properties. Fig. S1 (supplementary file) reveals a far more precise picture of the CZ-2 sample. This outcome confirms the presence of C-dots as well as ZnO phases in the CZnO-dots via XRD spectra investigation and matched with reported literature [13,27–29,31].

FTIR analyses were carried out to pin down the coordination between C-dots and ZnO and the surface functionalization of the as-prepared CZnO –dots, and it is shown in Fig. 2. The typical strong IR band of ZnO below 500 cm−1 matches the vibration mode of Zn–O bonding, confirming the formations of ZnO NPs [32]. The bare C-dots (Fig. S2) show the broadband at 3100–3400 cm−1 specifies the –OH stretching vibrations of water molecules. The bands at 2905 cm−1, 1407 cm−1, and 1028 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH bonds, aromatic C–N heterocycles, and epoxy C–O–C groups respectively [33]. The absorption band is positioned at 1582 cm−1 due to the C˭C (sp2 -hybridized carbon atoms) stretching vibration. Further in the CZnO-dots FTIR spectrum, the -OH stretching vibrations are weakly reduced compared to bare C-dots. It can be due to the addition of ZnO nanoparticles over the C-dots during the microwave pyrolysis for the CZnO-dots fabrication [34]. The absorption peak placed at 1740 cm−1, 1565 cm−1, 1395 cm−1,1035 cm−1 are indorsed to C˭O, C˭C, C-O, and C-O-C, respectively [23]. The new characteristic band below 500 cm−1 was perceived in addition to bare C-dots calling for Zn-O stretching vibrations [23,34]. These outcomes further evidence that the ZnO had been successfully incorporated to C-dots and are suitably in agreement with the literature studies [23,32–34].

XPS measurements were enacted for CZnO-dots (CZ-2 sample) to authorize the ZnO's surface attachment and chemical state. The noticeable scans were attained for C1s (284.6 eV), O1s (532.2 eV), Zn 2p3/2 (1022 eV), Zn 2p1/2 (1045 eV) to distinguish whether ZnO atoms as intertwined into C-dots and it is described in Fig. 3a. Fig. 3b displays the XPS spectrum of Zn at 2p orbital levels. The two peaks of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 orbital level binding energies originate at 1022 and 1045 eV. Additionally, subordinate binding energy peaks were witnessed at 12, 90, and 141 eV conforming to Zn 3d, Zn 3p, and Zn 3s electronic states, respectively [34]. The short scan of C1s deconvolution (Fig. 3c) revealed four C-binding sites at 282.5, 284.4, 286.1, 288.8 eV, which are resolved for C−Zn, C˭C, C-O, and C˭O, respectively [27]. In Fig. 3d, the O1s portrayal is fitted to three peaks locating at 529.5, 532.8, and 533.4 eV, showing Zn-O, C-OH/Zn-OH, and C˭O groups, respectively [27]. Specifically, the peak at 529.5 eV is possible for two types of bond formation, such as the carbon is directly bonded to Zn like O-Zn-C or else the carbon might indirectly chemically bonded to Zn as O-Zn-O-C through the other oxygen functionality groups of C- dots [35]. The existence of C and Zn in wide-ranging XPS spectrum and detection of no new compound between C, Zn, O atoms determine that ZnO atoms integrated into C-dots successfully. These XPS outcomes are in good concurrence with earlier literature reports [27,34,35].

Based on FESEM, the morphological topographies of the fabricated bare C-dots, pure ZnO, and CZnO-dots (CZ-2) were analyzed. The FESEM picture Fig. S3a displays bare C-dots with a quasi-spherical, non-uniform structure. The bare C-dots have a flat surface with a close-packed nanostructure. Similarly, Fig. S3b demonstrates FESEM photos of pure ZnO nanoparticles synthesized. ZnO NPs with an aggregated arrangement of small individual NPs are certainly spherical in form. The FESEM representations of the synthesized CZnO-dots are seen in Fig. S4a&b. The results indicate that individual ZnO nanoparticles fully cover the carbon surface, and the existence of ZnO particles with a spherical structure is seen in the high-resolution images. In Fig. S5a, the homogeneous pattern of nearly spherical ZnO NPs can be identified. Besides, for CZnO-dots, owing to ZnO NPs on the carbon surface, the surface roughness of the carbon increased significantly. The elemental scanning of CZnO-dots is seen in Fig. S5b and indicated the characteristic of carbon, oxygen, and Zn elements. The atomic % of each element present in the CZ-2 sample is tabulated in Table S1.

Using the HRTEM study, the morphological analysis of the size spectrum found in an as-fabricated CZ-2 sample was examined. Fig. 4 displays an overview TEM image of the CZnO-dots at different scale bars. The inset (Fig. 4a) histogram shows that the CZnO-dot particles are approximately within 1 and 6 nm in size range with the average size of the app. 2.6 nm. The region consists mainly of smaller particles and the sporadic detection of only larger particles. Fig. 4b inset image exhibits the SAED ring pattern of an area of tiny particles seen in the HRTEM images. Due to the small crystallite scale, the diffraction rings are enormous, and the ZnO ring is also hard to recognize. The lattice fringes were spotted in the HRTEM images of CZnO-dots in Fig. 4(c &d). The lattice spacing was estimated to be 0.25 nm and 0.45 nm for ZnO and C-dots, respectively [32]. The fringes pattern is well matched by the interlayer distance measured from XRD analysis. The findings described above suggested that the CZnO- dots were positively attained.

Fig. S6 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of bare C-dots with the absorption peak at 263 nm and the tail reaching into the visible range, which is reliable with the π–π* transition (C˭C bond) [34]. Fig. S7 displays the absorption spectrum of pure ZnO NPs, which depicts a broad absorption band at around 357 nm. The CZnO- dots (CZ-1, CZ-2, CZ-3, and CZ-4) samples unveil clear absorption in the visible region ( Fig. 5). The distinctive absorption peak around 350–360 nm specifies the presence of crystalline and impurity suppressed ZnO NPs [24]. The change in peaks in the absorption spectrum of UV–vis delivered strong evidence that the rate of cyclization of the CZnO-dots increased as the reaction proceeded. The absorption intensity increases with enhanced ZnO addition % in the CZnO-dots, comparable to previous reports that evaluated the ZnO/C-dots scheme [28,35,36]. Here, the presence of C-dots in CZnO-dots allows the intensity of visible light absorption to be increased. It can be due to an increase in the electrical charge of the surface of the oxides in CZnO-dots and the conversion in the basic mechanism of electron-hole pair formation during irradiation [35]. It is also evaluated as the prime reason for increased photocatalytic activity within the visible range. As seen, it is clear that the inclusion of ZnO would increase the visible light photocatalytic behavior of CZnO-dots [28].

The bandgap studies for the as-fabricated CZnO- dots samples, pure ZnO NPs, and bare C-dots were calculated using absorbance spectra employing tauc plot (relationship of (αhν) 2 vs photon energy). Band gaps for bare C-dots ( Fig. 6a) and pure ZnO (Fig. 6b) were 3.30 eV and 3.12 eV, further for CZnO- dots samples CZ-1, CZ-2, CZ-3, and CZ-4 samples were 2.93, 2.92, 2.98 and 3.03 eV respectively. It is important to note from the Fig. 6c-f that the bandgap is considerably reduced by 0.38 eV for CZ-2 relative to bare C-dots and 0.2 eV for pure ZnO NPs. This narrowing may be due to the involvement of Zn in the chemical bonding and the formation of a Zn-O-C bond compared to the earlier studies of ZnO-reduced graphene materials [37]. Further, the narrower bandgap accepts more light from longer wavelengths [37].

The fluorescence characteristics of the as-fabricated pure ZnO, bare C-dots, and CZnO-dots samples were studied in depth using a fluorescence spectrometer. The ZnO NPs emission is primarily classified as near band edge emission (NBE) in the UV region, including few emissions in the visible range. Oxygen interstitials (Oi), oxygen vacancies (VO), zinc interstitials (Zni), zinc vacancies (VZn) are the types of defects found in ZnO. The ZnO PL spectrum ( Fig. 7a) and the various defective energy states were evaluated using Gaussian deconvolution of ZnO NPs, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7a. Firstly, the NBE at 372 nm is obtained due to the recombination of excited electrons from localized states below the conduction band (CB) and holes from the valence band (VB), which is commonly regarded as the characteristic emission of ZnO [38].

Furthermore, the transitions from the CB to the holes localized at the defect stage associated with VZn is accountable for the violet emission at 414 nm (2.99 eV) [38]. Shifts from extended donor Zni states and holes in the VB trigger the blue luminescence bands at 430–480 nm (2.8–2.68 eV) [39]. The transition between the oxygen inertial (Oi) to the VB may be responsible for the blue-green emissions (480–510 nm). Because of the doping element, oxygen is lost, resulting in VO [39,40]. Furthermore, fundamental oxygen non-stoichiometry causes many oxygen defect sites on the integrated ZnO surface, which can act as electron trapping sites, making it ideal for practical photocatalytic applications.

Secondly, Fig. 7b shows the PL spectra of the bare C-dots solution. The emission peak of the C-dots redshifts from about 460–475 nm as the excitation wavelength ranges from 370 nm to 410 nm (inset of Fig. 7b). It's worth noting that the above-mentioned excitation-dependent emission is a common feature of C-dots [19]. In addition, PL spectra of CZnO-dots with various ratios are shown in Fig. S8a. All of the spectra display a dominant broad emission at around 460 nm, which has been observed frequently in bare C-dots PL and can be due to C-dot deep-level emission. The PL spectra of the CZnO-dots mixed solution have the same line form as C-dots, but the emission intensity differs with minor shifts in location, indicating that the hybrid system has good interaction between ZnO NPs and C-dots [41]. The observable PL intensity usually decreases as the ZnO content increases due to, first, scattering of the excitation radiation by surface-adsorbed ZnO atoms. Second, a non-radiative recombination process caused by ZnO impurity can eliminate PL emission.

In specific, the emission properties of the CZ-2 sample were examined by irradiating with different excitation wavelength [42]. The changes in fluorescence emission intensities of CZ-2 were seen in Fig. 7c at different excitations from 350 to 450 nm with an even interval wavelength of 10 nm. With a rising wavelength of 350 nm, the emission maximum shifted with decreasing intensity towards a longer wavelength. This investigation noticed the excitation-dependent property of the CZnO-dots. The maximum emission intensity was perceived at 465 nm at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 7d). The justification behind the excitation-dependent behavior of fluorescence may be ascribed to the diverse sizes of C-dots and the creation of new energy levels triggered by surface capturing states.

Consequently, the normalized emission of fluorescence in Fig. S8b indicates the behavior of excitation-dependent emission with redshift emission. Finally, samples of pure ZnO, bare C-dots, and CZnO-dots were compared. PL spectra reveal a redshift emission caused by interactions between C-dots and ZnO NPs, corroborating structural and morphological findings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CZnO-dots nanocatalyst efficiency towards MG dye decolourization
ce:section.ce:para: CZnO-dots are proficiently verified as photocatalyst for decolourization of MG dye under visible light. The outcome of various parameters such as ZnO addition %, CZnO-dots nanocatalyst loading, initial MG dye concentration, and pH range was evaluated using CZnO-dots under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different ZnO addition % in CZnO-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performances of the as-prepared bare C-dots, pure ZnO NPs, CZ-1, CZ-2, CZ-3, and CZ-4 samples were studied under visible light irradiation. The decolourization % of MG dye is calculated using Eq. (1). Improved photocatalytic efficiency was witnessed upon adding ZnO in C-dots than pure ZnO NPs and bare C-dots. In detail, the decolourization % of pure ZnO and bare C-dots was 46.4% and 67.3%, respectively. By contrast, when the ZnO was infused into C-dots, the decolourization efficiency was enhanced to 87.3% and 94.8% for CZ-1 and CZ-2, respectively. After attaining a maximum value of 94.8% for CZ-2, the degradation efficiency declined with the further increase of the ZnO addition % ( Fig. 8a). It may be due to the formation of additional oxygen vacancies, and the upsurge ZnO addition % offers recombination centers for photoinduced e-/h+ [38]. Fig. S9 illustrates the kinetic degradation data fitted by a pseudo-first-order equation with a basic Langmuir-Hinshelwood model from Eq. (2). The constant rate values of bare C-dots, pure ZnO, CZ-1, CZ-2, CZ-3, and CZ-4 values were tabulated in Table S2. It was encountered that the rate constant for the CZ-2 sample (kMG= 0.0466/min) was thrice more significant than that of pure ZnO (kMG = 0.01927/min) and four times higher than that of bare C-dots (kMG=0.01072). The bar graph of the MG dye solution degradation kinetics with bare C-dots, pure ZnO NPs, and CZ-2 under visible light irradiation at a time interval is also displayed in Fig. S10.

The effect of pH is a significant criterion in photocatalytic decolourization since it corresponds to the evidence of nanocatalyst surface charge properties. [38,43]. Fig. 8b shows the impact of various pH (3, 6, 8, 10, and 12) ranges on the MG dye degradation efficiency. For the experiment, the pH value was regulated using a dilute HCl or NaOH solution. The decolourization % of the fixed amount of CZ-2 nanocatalyst (1.5 mg) on degrading 0.05 g/L of MG dye at diverse pH - 3, 6, 8, 10, and 12 was scrutinized. The maximum efficiency (94%) was observed at pH 8, and the minimum efficiency (34%) was noted at pH 3. The plausible motive of this alteration can be clarified based on the surface charge of the CZ-2 nanocatalyst in which MG dye molecules get adsorbed. Under acidic conditions, the surface of CZ-2 is positively charged, which reduces the active sites required for cationic dye adsorption, such as MG dye. Instead, in alkaline pH, the CZ-2 surface is negatively charged, accelerating the absorption of MG dye and its self-sensitized degradation [44,45]. The maximum decolourization of MG dye is then accomplished at pH-8, which degrades the dye up to 94% beneath the visible light time of 60 min.

Catalyst loading is a significant parameter for obtaining an effective photocatalytic decolourization process. The increase in catalyst quantity doubles the level of active sites on the surface of the catalyst, thus increasing hydroxyl (•OH) and superoxide (•O2) radical generation [46].

Therefore, to know the relationship between CZnO-dots (CZ-2) catalyst loading and the photocatalytic efficiency, MG dye decolourization of 0.05 g/L using CZ-2 under visible irradiation was investigated. Fig. 9a shows the increasing measures of the prepared CZ-2 nanocatalyst loading in the series of 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, and 2.5 mg used at constant pH 8. It is noted that increasing the nanocatalyst measure from 0.5 to 1.5 mg, and the degradation efficiency augmented from 88% to 94%. It was ascribed to the upsurge in the sum of active sites extant on the CZ-2 surface. The MG dye decolourization decreases to 89% and 87% as the amount of CZ-2 catalyst is amended to 2 and 2.5 mg. This trend is accountable for two reasons; firstly, the turbidity caused in the solution congested the light penetration to the MG dye solution. Secondly, while increasing the amount of nanocatalyst, nanoparticle agglomeration can occur, which reduces the surface area of CZ-2, so it decreases the degradation efficiency [23]. Consequently, to deteriorate the given pollutant, there is a limit of catalyst amount to be used, beyond which the rate of degradation gradually decreases [38]. Therefore, the optimum CZ-2 catalyst dose was 1.5 mg to decolourize the MG dye solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the initial MG dye concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the impact of MG dye initial concentration on the CZ-2 nanocatalyst, a different set of experiments are carried out by adding a fixed amount of CZ-2 nanocatalyst (1.5 mg) with diverse initial dye concentration from 0.04 to 0.08 g/L upholding the dye solution at pH 8. The decolourization efficiency for various initial MG dye concentrations is plotted, and it is shown in Fig. 9b. The maximum MG dye decolourization under visible light was achieved at 0.05 g/L. At first, the % efficiency rises slightly with an increasing MG dye concentration.

It may be because of the following explanation. The possibility of •OH radical formation on the catalyst surface and •OH radicals reacting with dye molecules determines the degradation rate in most cases. As the initial dye concentrations rise, more dye molecules are needed for excitation and energy transfer. The formation of multiple monolayers of adsorbed dye on the CZnO-dot surface, favored at high dye concentrations, could explain this dependence [47]. Since the surface is not entirely covered until the critical level is reached, reaction rates or % efficiency remain constant [38,43,47]. However, this trend is reversed when further increases in dye concentration which decreases the catalyst activity. It might be attributed to reaction suppression because more dye molecules are adsorbed on the catalyst surface at high dye concentration, reducing the •OH radical formation [48]. Or else, a fixed catalyst proportion provides a fixed amount of reactive radicals that is not sufficient to deteriorate the higher dye concentration [49].

Comparison of the decolourization % of MG dye with different light conditions was studied. (a) dark condition + without CZ-2 nanocatalyst (b) dark condition + with CZ-2 nanocatalyst, (c) MG + Visible light, and (d) CZ-2 nanocatalyst + MG + visible light, under the optimum condition (0.05 g/L of MG dye solution kept at pH-8 with 1.5 mg of CZ-2 nanocatalyst in a time interval of 60 min) is shown in Fig. S11. No significant dye decolourization was observed for all dark conditions, and a minor decolourization of up to 2% was observed under visible light irradiation without a catalyst. The synthesized CZnO-dots nanocatalyst is, therefore, a favorable candidate for MG photocatalytic activity.

The CZ-2 nanocatalyst was recycled three times with no discernible loss of decolourization efficiency. Before reloading, the used CZ-2 nanocatalyst was centrifuged, rinsed with ethanol and water, and dried in an oven for two hours at 100 °C. The optimized dye concentration and catalyst loading were used for the reusability study. As a result of the catalyst getting lost during centrifugation, the % efficiency could have decreased slightly. It specifies that CZ-2 exhibits good stability and reusability as a photocatalyst ( Fig. 10a).

Radical scavengers were established to trap exact reactive species engaged in MG dye decolourization under visible light. Trap study trials have been conducted by employing scavengers like ethanol (for •OH radical) [50], NaCl (for h+) [23], Ascorbic acid (for the superoxide •O2 – radicals) [51], and the K2Cr2O7 (for e-) [51] to MG solution. The scavenger's concentration was fixed at 1 mM, and the optimized experimental conditions were used for these studies. Fig. 10b shows their efficiency % of the scavengers participating in the process of photo decolourization. In detail, without any scavengers, decomposition of MG dye was perceived up to 94.8%. There is significant suppression in MG dye degradation upon the addition of ethanol, NaCl, K2Cr2O7, and ascorbic acid, which shows the efficiency of 69.23%, 53.85%, 16.24%, and 8.55%, respectively. The findings showed that under visible light, •OH, h+, •O2- and e- played a crucial role in the MG dye decolourization process. However, the superoxide radicals and electrons were the ultimate leading species in the MG - CZnO-dots system. These findings were also consistent with the work of Sharma et al. [23] on N doped ZnO/C-dot nanoflowers for the degradation of MG dye in which electrons were found to be the topmost predator in the photocatalytic degradation of MG dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The plausible mechanism of MG dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible mechanism of C-dot-based systems is explained in three necessary steps (a) the absorption of light contributes to forming pairs of e−/h+. (b) The partition and shift of e−/h+ pairs lead to the creation of reactive species. (c) The produced reactive species lead to a significant photocatalytic reaction [50]. In this manner, the improved CZnO-dots photocatalytic activity specifies the vital part of C-dots in the photocatalytic system. The synergic property of ZnO and C-dots can significantly increase the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and upsurge the transfer percentage of e−/h+ pairs [23]. The charge separation efficiency is credited due to C-dots' rapid photoinduced charge transfer behavior that obstructs the e−/h+ pairs recombination [50]. Efficient charging isolation may enhance the life of the charge carrier and increase the efficiency of the transfer of the interfacial charge to the adsorbed dye molecules, accounting for the CZnO-dots enhanced photocatalytic activity [37].

According to the trapping studies, the proposed mechanism of the visible-light-responsive MG dye photocatalytic degradation using CZnO-dots is diagrammatically explained in Fig. 11. In the photocatalytic process, the ZnO acts as the e- donor and generates the e- upon light absorption. The excited e-s is shifted to the CB of the C-dots. Consequently, the electrons were detached from ZnO and suppress the e−/h+ recombination. Additionally, the photogenerated electrons (e-) captures the dissolved oxygen on the C-dots surface and creates superoxide radicals (•O2−), which are responsive species to degrade MG dye. Photosensitized h+s that were enduring in the VB of ZnO respond with water molecules to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH) or straightly interrelate with MG dye molecules organic radicals. Simultaneously, the reactive oxygen species may also intermingle with MG molecules and decompose them [50]. Table 1 displays the various green source utilized for synthesizing metal/metal Oxide-C-dots-based photocatalyst for photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treated dye solution for the Mung bean plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by Gunture et al. [57], an attempt was made to reuse the treated dye solution to verify the plant growth's aptness and sustainability by performing an eco-toxicological bioassay. For this, the fast-growing plant, mung bean seeds were taken as the model. Germinated mung bean seeds were grown with MG dye, photodegraded CZnO-dots, and the influence of the growth was witnessed after the 15 days of germination, as revealed in Fig. 12. It is observed that the MG dye solution suppressed plant growth, but the mung bean plants were grown with treated dye solution exhibited a nearly similar way of development associated with the control plants (treated with DDW and in CZnO-dots). Interestingly, CZnO-dots facilitated plant growth like in the case of DDW, which firmly supported non-toxic nature. The results obtained are in favor of to use of the treated dye solution. This method could inspire the reuse of treated dye solution moderately to water the play area, parks, and roadways garden to further reduce natural water consumption. But more accurate and comprehensive studies have to be taken care of for the edible plants.

In a nutshell, the CZnO-dots photocatalysts have been well fabricated and examined to support the occurrence of ZnO and C-dots portions. The MG dye decolourization by CZnO-dots photocatalysts was scrutinized methodically via various parameters like catalyst dosage, dye concentration, and different pH. The CZnO-dots photocatalytic active conditions in the experiment were found to be: 1.5 mg catalyst degraded 94.8% of 0.5 g/L MG dye at pH 8 in 60 min under visible light. The scavenging studies explored the pathways regulating the influential photocatalytic behavior of CZnO-dots by which superoxides radicals and electrons were accountable for the MG dye decolourization. Besides, the use of treated dye water for mung bean plants' development could be considered sustainable for hands-on applications and effective remediation to dye-loaded effluents.
No funds, grants, or other support was received.
